coredata.dc:title: PEG mediated NiMn2O4 nanomaterials as a nano catalyst for peroxidase mimetic activity and photocatalytic degradation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Shahanas, T.
Harichandran, G.
coredata.dc:description: Artificial peroxidases have garnered a lot of attention owing to their tremendous superiority over their natural counterparts. Here, NiMn2O4 nanoparticles have been successfully prepared through PEG assisted hydrothermal method. The varied PEG concentrations significantly altered the morphology and particle size of the synthesizedmaterials. We demonstrate the improved peroxide-like assay of different NiMn2O4 nanoparticles for the first time. Among them, Ni4 nanoparticles exhibit good peroxidase-like activity by generating the oxidation of chromogenic substrate 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 and a blue color charge transfer product with an absorption maximum is positioned at 652 nm. These observations led to the development of a method for assessingH2O2 that can be read visually and photometrically. The Ni4 nanoparticles show enhanced kinetics compared to the natural enzyme horse radish peroxidase (HRP) with a lower Km (0.168 mM) value. Additionally, this Ni4 nanosphere applies as a visible light photocatalyst for the degradation of methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (Rh B) dyes under visible-light irradiation. Under optimized conditions, the degradationrates of MB and Rh B are 68 and 80.7 %, respectively, after 210 min, and recyclable efficiency is about 99 % for Rh B photocatalytic degradation in the first test and 98 % for five cycles, and about 98 % for MB photocatalytic degradation in the first test and 97 % for five cycles.

head.ce:title: PEG mediated NiMn2O4 nanomaterials as a nano catalyst for peroxidase mimetic activity and photocatalytic degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, Chennai 600 025, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer Science, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, Chennai 600 025, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Artificial peroxidases have garnered a lot of attention owing to their tremendous superiority over their natural counterparts. Here, NiMn2O4 nanoparticles have been successfully prepared through PEG assisted hydrothermal method. The varied PEG concentrations significantly altered the morphology and particle size of the synthesizedmaterials. We demonstrate the improved peroxide-like assay of different NiMn2O4 nanoparticles for the first time. Among them, Ni4 nanoparticles exhibit good peroxidase-like activity by generating the oxidation of chromogenic substrate 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 and a blue color charge transfer product with an absorption maximum is positioned at 652 nm. These observations led to the development of a method for assessingH2O2 that can be read visually and photometrically. The Ni4 nanoparticles show enhanced kinetics compared to the natural enzyme horse radish peroxidase (HRP) with a lower Km (0.168 mM) value. Additionally, this Ni4 nanosphere applies as a visible light photocatalyst for the degradation of methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (Rh B) dyes under visible-light irradiation. Under optimized conditions, the degradationrates of MB and Rh B are 68 and 80.7 %, respectively, after 210 min, and recyclable efficiency is about 99 % for Rh B photocatalytic degradation in the first test and 98 % for five cycles, and about 98 % for MB photocatalytic degradation in the first test and 97 % for five cycles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Artificial enzymes made of nanoparticles, also known as “nanozymes,” have attracted much attention in recent years due to their capacity to imitate the activity of natural enzymes while also overcoming some of their disadvantages. Natural enzymes have been regarded as proteins and widely used as one of the key components in POC biosensor advancement due to their highly effective biocatalytic behavior, which can catalyze a variety of specific chemical or biochemical reactions and result in the generation of colorimetric or electrochemical signals in the presence of analytes with ideal performance and selectivity [14]. Natural enzymes such as glucose oxidase [15], alcohol oxidase [16], lactate oxidase [17], lactate dehydrogenase [18], urate oxidase [19], horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [20], and cytochrome c reductase [21] have all been employed for various biosensing applications. Nevertheless, natural enzymes have a few traits that prevent them from being used in biosensing in a wider variety: limited physiological circumstances at work, diminished stability in challenging conditions, excessive manufacturing costs, and operational complications in manufacturing. Consequently, there has been a surge in research into “artificial enzymes” or enzyme mimics over the past few years. Numerous applications can be made of the nanoparticles' capability to mimic enzyme activity, particularly peroxidase mimics. Moreover, the majority of these nanozymes can resemble natural enzymes in key enzymatic reactions, namely for the oxidation of the chromogenic substrate, peroxidase [22] and for the oxidation of the chromogenic substrate, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) [23]. Activated H2O2 acts as an electron donor and undertakes alkaline decomposition on the surface of nanoparticles, which then aides the development of OH and oxygen superoxide anion (O2 –. ), according to the most commonly recognized mechanism for the peroxidase-like activity of nanoparticles (metals) implemented in an acidic environment. The following oxidation of the peroxidase substrates caused by the . OH and O2 .- radicals would lead to the emergence of colored compounds [24–26]. Over the years, several inorganic nanoparticles have been employed as peroxidase mimetics, including ZnO-Pd [27], Fe2O3 [28], Cu0.89Zn0.11O [29], Fe-doped MoS2 [30], PbWO4 [31], SnO2-Cu [32], CuO [33], ZnO [34], Co3O4 [35], and so on.Interestingly, to the best of our exploration, no applications of a peroxidase mimic analysis for Nickel Manganese Oxide detection have been published as of yet.

Particle size has a significant impact on how well nano-photocatalytic materials function. The photocatalytic reaction rate drops rapidly as the basic particle size increases [36]. Koci et al. have demonstrated that the specific surface area, charge-carrier dynamics, and light absorption efficacy of TiO2 nanoparticles all altered as the particle size varied [37]. According to Cheng et al., the AgI/BiOI photocatalysts exhibit size-dependent photocatalytic activity that rises with decreasing AgI NP size [38]. These studies have demonstrated that catalyst characteristics are significantly influenced by particle size. Therefore, the synthesis of photocatalysts with controllable morphology and size provided a wide range of options for adjusting versatility and developing photocatalytic properties. Widespread use of polyethylene glycol as a powerful surfactant agent in the generation of metal nanoparticles [39]. According to recent studies, PEG 6000 proved excellent at regulating the size and form of metal nanoparticles. The surface moderation of these nanoparticles is crucial to improving their application to photocatalytic applications [40]. Photocatalysts should have large surface areas and possible control of particle shape and size by varying the concentration of stabilizers [41]. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) is a potential candidate to work as a stabilizer. As it can minimize agglomeration, this particular PEG is a wise option [42–45].

The removal of RhB and MB from industrial effluent has been the subject of numerous investigations. Using semiconductor nanostructures in photocatalysis to degrade both inorganic and organic contaminants has become a promising method [46]. A photocatalyst is a semiconducting substance that generates electron-hole pairs when exposed to light. The crucial characteristics of a photocatalyst for a photocatalytic system include surface area, shape, bandgap, band edge, and charge carriers [47]. Manganese oxides, one of these metal oxides, offer excellent potential for wastewater cleanup. Moreover, manganese dioxide has good catalytic potential even at low temperatures [48]. NiMn2O4 nanomaterial is now being used to remove the dyes MB and RB from water. The higher photocatalytic efficiency of NiMn2O4 nanomaterial under visible light irradiation is anticipated, and the hydroxyl radical (.OH) produced in the Fenton reaction can lead to the mineralization and degradation of a variety of organic compounds [49]. It is well established that nanosphere morphology helps the photocatalytic performance of material by increasing its surface area and giving it large adsorptive active sites, photosensitization, bandgap tuning, and a reduction in the rate of electron-hole recombination [50].

In this study, we demonstrate a facile hydrothermal technique for the synthesis of NiMn2O4 nanoparticles. The morphology of the synthesized material is regulated by adjusting the quantity of PEG-6000 used as a surfactant. As a peroxidase mimic to detect H2O2, four different NiMn2O4 materials with different particle sizes and morphologies were used. A quick and effective process for the colorimetric monitoring of H2O2 was expanded to include the remarkable inherent peroxidase-like activity of Ni4 nanoparticles. An investigation of the catalytic kinetics of the enzyme, similar to the peroxidase catalytic mechanism of Ni4 nanoparticles, was also carried out. And another attempt has been made to use Ni4 nanoparticles as photocatalysts for the degradation of Rhodamine B dye and Methylene Blue under Visible light irradiation.

TMB (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Ni(No3)2·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2. XH2O, Diethyl amine, PEG-6000, Methylene Blue (MB), Rhodamine B (Rh B), ethanol, H2O2 (30%), isopropanol (IPA, a scavenger of hydroxyl radicals), and other chemicals were all of the analytical reagent grades and were bought from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL India). All the chemicals and reagents are directly used without any purification. The preparation of all aqueous solutions used double-distilled water.

NiMn2O4 nanoparticles were synthesized using a PEG-mediated hydrothermal process. The PEG solution was made by dissolving 0.02 g of PEG in 10 mL of distilled water with ongoing stirring for an hour. In this procedure, 70 mL of distilled water was used to dissolve 0.04 M of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and Mn(NO3)2.XH2O, which were then carefully mixed for 30 min. Following that, the synthesized precursor solutions were combined for a further two hours with PEG solution (0.02 g in 10 mL DW) while being constantly stirred. Finally, while the solution was being continuously stirred, 2 mL of Diethyl amine was added drop by drop. The resulting composition was then put into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for 24 h at 180 °C. The precipitate was collected and frequently rinsed with distilled water and ethanol after the process. The acquired precipitate was annealed for three hours at 400 °C after being thoroughly dried at 70 °C. The resulting precipitate is termed Ni2 nanoparticles. PEG concentrations of 0.02, 0.03, and 0.04 g have been employed to synthesize Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4 nanoparticles, respectively, and Ni1 was prepared without the use of a PEG template.

The peroxidase substrate TMB is catalytically oxidized in the presence of H2O2 to investigate the peroxidase-like behavior of the NiMn2O4 materials at room temperature. In a typical experiment, 300 μL of 1.0 mg⋅mL−1 Ni4 nanoparticles were dissolved in 400 μL of 50 mM phosphate buffer (pH = 4.0), including 200 μL of 10.0 mM H2O2 and 100 μL of 8.0 mM TMB. Directly upon addition and mixing of all compounds, reactions were observed in a time-scan mode at 652 nm using a Lab India Analytics UV–Vis Spectrophotometer 2000U. The control groups consisted of Ni4 nanoparticles (300 μg/mL) with TMB (0.8 mM), Ni4 nanoparticles (300 μg/mL) with H2O2 (2.0 mM), and TMB (0.8 mM) with H2O2 (2.0 mM) in 20 mM phosphate buffer (pH = 4.0). To determine the ideal conditions, the influence of the H2O2 concentration (0–0.1 M), Ni4 concentration (0–200 μg/mL), temperature (20–40 °C), and pH (2–8) on the peroxidase mimetic action of Ni4 nanoparticles was also examined. Active species caging experiments were conducted with 10.0 mM IPA (a quencher of OH) and 10.0 mM BQ (a quencher of O2) added to the reaction solutions, respectively, with other experimental circumstances held constant, in order to look into radicals that are important in the catalytic process [51,52].

In a 3.0 mL reaction solution (20.0 mM PBS, pH = 3.0, 25 °C) with 20 μg/mL Ni4 nanoparticles, a steady-state kinetic test was conducted. As substrates, TMB and H2O2 were used in the process. By altering the ratio of one substrate while maintaining the concentration of another substrate, steady-state kinetic data were obtained. The kinetic parameter was then evaluated in time trend mode at 652 nm [53,54]. Employing the formula: 1/v = (Km/Vmax) (1/[S]) + 1/Vmax [42,43], where v has been the beginning velocity, Km would be the Michaelis constant, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, and [S] is the substrate concentration, the Michaelis-Menten constant was derived.

The degradation of MB or Rh B in an aqueous solution served as evidence of the catalytic activity of Ni4 nanosphere materials. The preparation of MB and RB solutions involved adding 0.015 mg of Ni4 nanosphere material to 50 mL of MB or Rh B aqueous solution (10 mg/L) and then separately adding 10 mL of aqueous H2O2 (30 wt%%) to the reaction solution. A small quantity of the mixed solution was swiftly separated at short intervals. To evaluate the catalytic degradation of MB or Rh B, using a quartz cell and a pipet, supernatants were analyzed using a UV–Visible spectrophotometer [54,55].

Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154056 nm) was used to gather the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns using a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker Inc., Karlsruhe, Germany). Employing a Hitachi S-4800N microscope (Hitachi Inc., Tokyo, Japan), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were examined. The JEOL JEM-2100 microscope apparatus (JEOL Inc., Tokyo, Japan) was used to capturee transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) pictures at a 200 kV acceleration voltage. The JEOL JEM-2100 microscope apparatus (JEOL Inc., Tokyo, Japan) was used to capturee transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) pictures at a 200 kV acceleration voltage. Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy was used to examine the chemical components and elemental maps of the samples as prepared utilizing the EDX attachment to the SEM instrument. The specific surface areas of NiMn2O4 were measured relying on the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation by an automatic nitrogen adsorption pore size distribution and specific surface analyzer (ASAP 2460, Atlanta, Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra of dye solutions during photocatalytic degradations were recorded at room temperature on a Lab India Analytics UV–Vis Spectrophotometer 2000U.

The XRD patterns of prepared materials Ni1, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4 are displayed in Fig. 1 , and it was determined that the samples are distinct groups of peaks that can be precisely compared to a common pattern of Nickel manganese oxide (JCPDS no. 00-001-1110) with normal spinel structure (space group Fd-3 m) and lattice constants of a = b = c = 8.382 Å. The lattice planes (111), (311), (400), (422), and (440) are closely matched with the diffraction peak sites, which were obtained at 2θ angles of 18.27, 35.45, 43.25, 53.54, and 62.72°, respectively. Based on the diffraction peak full-width half maximum (FWHM), the average crystallite size was used to get the Scherrer equation [56].
(1)

where λ is the wavelength of the Cu Kα X-ray is 2.2897 and β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak. The average crystallite sizes were found to be 9.51, 7.87, 5.32, 21.74, and 6.91 nm for Ni1, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4, respectively.

The recorded FT-IR spectra of synthesized Ni1, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4 materials additionally affirmed the development of NiMn2O4. As seen in Fig. 2 , the FTIR spectra of Ni1, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4 generally show three distinct peaks near 602, 471, and 424 cm−1. The signal 423 cm−1 is caused by nickel species (Ni-O) vibrating in octahedral sites [57]. The peak of just about 471 cm−1 is ascribed to the vibration of the Mn3+ at the octahedral site and the band at 602 cm−1 was related to the intrinsic stretching vibration of atoms at the tetrahedral site (M–O) [58].

To calculate the surface area (m2/g), pore volume (cm3/g), and average pore diameter (nm) of the synthesized Ni11, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method and the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm at 77 K were performed, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 , it can be observed that all samples exhibited a type IV isotherm with an H3 hysteresis loop. The Surface area, total pore volume, and average pore diameter measured from N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm curves for Ni11, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4 nanoparticles are displayed in Table 1 . The Ni1, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4 materials have distinct surface areas of 14.02, 16.73, 22.14, and 24.60 m2g−1, respectively. In addition, pore volumes of 0.033, 0.039, 0.052, and 0.058 cm3g−1 were obtained for Ni1, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4 materials, respectively. Despite all the materials, Ni4 offers the highest surface area (24.60 m2g−1) and pore volume (0.058 cm3g−1). Due to its larger surface area, the Ni4 material has channels and sites, which might induce the occurrence of photo-generated charge carriers and increase visible light, which would impact the amount of photocatalytic performance.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) provideskey facts regarding the surface electronic states and the more precise elemental composition of the NiMn2O4 nanomaterials, and the relevant outcomes are shown in Fig. 4 . A full wide-scan spectrum (Fig. 4a) of NiMn2O4 nanomaterials indicates the presence of Ni, Mn, and O and the absence of other impurities. The Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 4b) shows that two major peaks located at 854 eV and 855.7 eV can be assigned to Ni 2p3/2 and the peak at 873.1 eV corresponds to Ni 2p1/2, respectively. The satellite (Sat.) peaks of Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 are two peaks with binding energies that are situated at 861 eV and 879.1 eV, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 4b. The Mn spectrum of the NiMn2O4 nanomaterialis shown in Fig. 4c. In the Mn spectra, two spin–orbit peaks are deconvoluted into four peaks. At 641.8 and 643.7 eV, two deconvolved peaks are seen; these peaks match Mn 2p3/2. Mn 2p1/2 is represented by two deconvoluted peaks at 653.1 and 654.2 eV, which are compatible with previously published data. Two deconvoluted peaks, which relate to the concomitant peaks of Mn2+, are seen at 641.8 and 653.1 eV, and two deconvoluted peaks, which belong to the concomitant peaks of Mn3+ binding energy, are shown at 643.7 and 654.2 eV. Fig. 4d shows the O spectrum of NiMn2O4 nanomaterial. The recovered peak is attributed to the lattice oxygen at a binding energy of 529.7 eV. Metal-O–H from hydroxyl groups on metal surfaces is considered to be the cause of the peak for O 1s at 531.1 eV [56–58].

Furthermore, earlier studies have shown a connection between the catalytic behavior of a catalyst and band gap energy. Along with other characteristics, we calculated the energy band gap of NiMn2O4 materials with various PEG concentrations. The band energy gaps of the as-prepared samples could be estimated by using Eq. (2) [59].
(2)

α is the absorption coefficient, A is the variable that is connected to the effective masses of the valence and conduction bands, hν is the absorption energy, and Eg represents the band gap energy, respectively. The extrapolated intercept correlating to the Eg value in Fig. 5 is obtained by plotting (hν) 1/2 versus hν based on the spectral response using the Tauc equation. The band gap of the prepared NiMn2O4 nanoparticles was obtained at 2.25, 1.89, 1.80, and 1.73 eV, respectively, for Ni1, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4. Interestingly, Ni4 has a larger surface area of 24.60 m2 g−1, which is the key factor in its improved photocatalytic activity. As a result, the increased surface area enables more visible light to create more photo-generated charge carriers, which will influence the degree of photocatalytic activity. Note that Ni4 has a lower band gap than Ni1, Ni2, and Ni3, but due to its higher surface area, it may expect to show higher photocatalytic activity [60]. The decrease in band gap is attributed to the enhanced photocatalytic activity. Taking into account the energy band gap and the activities of the samples revealed that there is no direct correlation between the two for these prepared materials. The maximum catalytic activity was demonstrated by Ni4 with the lowest Eg, whereas Ni1 with the highest Eg had the lowest activity.

The morphology of the prepared materials was characterized by FE-SEM and TEM. Fig. 6 shows representative FE SEM images of NiMn2O4 prepared with different weight proportions of PEG. As per Fig. 6(a–h), the Ni1 sample was typically non-uniform, lacked a recognizable shape, and measured approximately at 74 nm, or near a micrometer in size. The Ni2 sample (0.02 g PEG) is depicted in Fig. 6(b,f) in both low- and high-magnification FE-SEM images, which indicate a clump of aggregated structure with an average size of 62 nm. The clump of the agglomerated structure started to change after the PEG content was increased from 0.02 to 0.03 g (Ni3), and a few of the particles were fractal patterns, as seen in Fig. 6(c,g). In comparison to the Ni1 and Ni2 specimens, these architectures may be expected to provide more catalytic active sites. Interestingly, the shape of the synthesized NiMn2O4 changed to a spherical-like morphology when the concentration of PEG was raised to 0.04 g (Ni4) (Fig. 6(d, h)), resulting in extremely homogenous and well-defined nanoparticles with diameters between 41 nm. These nanoparticles are reported to demonstrate improved catalytic activity in the oxidation of TMB because of their structured form and tunable sizes. When PEG is unused, the agglomerated block-like morphology is formed. In conclusion, nanosphere morphology benefited from the relatively large concentration of PEG and its size of about nm. From the above results, it is concluded that PEG is considered to have been crucial in the formation of nanoparticles. The catalytic activity is significantly influenced by the catalyst particle. Smaller particle sizes exhibit improved catalytic performance. The nanosphere morphology and particle size of Ni4 are greatly influenced by the PEG weight ratio. The sample with the lowest weight ratio has an agglomerated shape and a diameter that is almost a micrometer in size. With the increase in PEG content, the nanoparticles become more uniform, and the number of active sites on the unit surface area is controlled identically. The differences in catalytic performance must be caused by the diameter of the support. And meanwhile, the average diameters decrease and change to about 41 nm for the sample with a 0.4 g weight ratio of PEG. Small particle sizes are more beneficial to the diffusion of the reagent molecules. As a result, the smaller the particle size, the faster the reagent and product spread, and the better the catalytic performance becomes.

HR-TEM analysis further enhances the nanospherical morphology of the Ni4 material. The Ni4 material is shown in HR-TEM images at lower and higher magnifications in Fig. 7 (a–c), which reveal homogenous nano-spherical shapes and are much more consistent with HR-SEM findings. The lattice fringes of Ni4 material are shown in Fig. 7d, with an interplanar distance of 0.209 nm matching the (400) plane of NiMn2O4. The prepared Ni4 suggested a polycrystalline nature and correlated with XRD data. Furthermore, Fig. 7e illustrates the atomic composition (%) proportions of the Ni4 and verifies that Ni, Mn, and O have respective atomic composition (%) ratios of 11.25%, 22.26%, and 66.51%.

To evaluate the peroxidase-mimicking activity of the synthesized Ni4 nanoparticles illustrated in Fig. 8 a, the typical peroxidase substrate TMB was catalytically oxidized with the inclusion or exclusion of H2O2. At a wavelength of 652 nm, the absorbance variation of the reaction was observed in time-scan mode. A typical experiment was carried out with 400 mL of 50 mM phosphate buffer (pH = 4.0), 300 mL of 1.0 μg/mL Ni4 nanoparticles, 200 mL of 10.0 mM H2O2, and 100 mL of 8.0 mM TMB. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the reaction system were measured in the region of 400–800 nm on a UV–Visible spectrometer. Color changes from different systems were shown in Fig. 8b. Fig. 8a demonstrates that the systems of H2O2 + Ni4 and H2O2 + TMB do not absorb any light, whereas the systems of TMB + H2O2 + Ni4 exhibit an intense peak of absorption at 652 nm, suggesting that Ni4 nanosphere material may work as a peroxidase to oxidize TMB and generate a blue color when H2O2 is present. Though the other control tests demonstrated that the TMB alone or in combination with H2O2 did not significantly alter the color of the solution, It’s interesting to note that the TMB + Ni4 combination exhibits a modest absorption, which suggests that NiMn2O4 could catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the absence of H2O2 to form a blue dye, which was most likely due to the oxidase-emulating behavior of NiMn2O4 materials. The capacity to catalytically decrease the dissolved oxygen may be the source of the oxidase-like activity for TMB oxidation [61].

Additionally, the catalytic oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 was used to test and compare the peroxidase mimetic catalytic activity of prepared NiMn2O4 at various concentrations of PEG surfactant, as shown in Fig. 8c. All of the NiMn2O4 samples that were synthesized at various PEG concentrations were found to have specific peroxidase-mimetic catalytic activity. Fig. 8d shows the noticeable color changes in various reaction systems with varying concentrations of PEG. Among all samples, the [H2O2 + TMB + Ni4] spherical system exhibited an apparent dark blue color, whereas the [H2O2 + TMB + Ni1], [H2O2 + TMB + Ni2], and the [H2O2 + TMB + Ni3] systems were almost light colored. The exceptional peroxidase mimic activity of Ni4 nanoparticles may be primarily linked to their high specific surface area and abundance of catalytic activity sites. Additionally, because of the substantial BET-specific surface area (24.60 m2g−1), many TMB molecules will be deposited on the surfaces of Ni4 nanoparticles. Collectively, they increased the rate of the reaction, improved electron transport, and raised the catalytic properties of NiMn2O4, giving it better peroxidase-like activity. Instead, less negatively charged Ni1, Ni2, and Ni3 (having a minimal BET-specific surface area) do not catalyze the oxidation of TMB as compared with Ni4 nanoparticles. These observations revealed a correlation between the peroxidase-like capability of the NiMn2O4 materials and the BET-specific surface areas of 14.02, 16.73, 22.14, and 24.60 m2g−1, respectively, for Ni1, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4. Furthermore, during the same reaction time, the absorbance of the Ni4 system is significantly higher than that of the Ni1, Ni2, and Ni3 systems. This could be explained by the two amino groups found in TMB, which most likely result in a stronger affinity for the more negatively charged Ni4 surface. More adversely, here Electrostatically, Ni4 has the propensity to pull amino groups from TMB, and it exhibits substantial catalytic performance toward TMB [62]. As a result, compared to Ni1, Ni2, and Ni3 systems, Ni4 nanoparticles showed greater catalytic performance on the oxidation of TMB with H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase mimetic nano enzymic activity of Ni4 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of H2O2-containing solutions to liberate active species.OH and O2.-, which are crucial for the catalytic process, are widely acknowledged. The decomposition of H2O2 into ·OH, as per previous studies, was the main cause of the peroxidase-like activity. An experiment with OH capture was done to verify this mechanism. The generated ·OH is oxidizing the TMB to yield a blue product [52,63]. Therefore, studies involving the trapping of radicals were done to examine the peroxidase-like catalytic mechanism of Ni4 nanospherical material. TMB was chosen as the catalytic substrate since it was a characteristic chromogenic substrate for peroxidase and to test the NiMn2O4 nanoparticles' capability to mimic peroxidase. In the presence of H2O2, NiMn2O4 can react as a Fenton-like catalyst and catalyze the oxidation of the peroxidase substrate TMB, yielding a blue-colored product [64]. The reduction of H2O2 initially occurs in the presence of the chromogenic electron donor TMB, as seen in Scheme 1 (Fig. 9 ), and then it is enhanced by partial electron transfer to the surface of Ni4. A sudden color change is seen when TMB, H2O2, and Ni4 are mixed, as illustrated in Fig. 9, with the greatest absorbance of 652 nm. This color shift is the result of the colorless TMB being oxidized into its blue byproduct. TMB ox through one electron transfer catalyzed by the spherically arranged Ni4 NP in the presence of H2O2 [65]. There is no noticeable color reaction in the absence of TMB or H2O2. It should be noted that under the same circumstances, TMB can also be slowly oxidized by H2O2 without the use of a catalyst. As a consequence, the H2O2 and TMB systems also exhibit a modest color shift. The slow breakdown of the H2O2 substrate into hydroxyl radicals, which can then catalyze the oxidation of TMB, may be the cause of this phenomenon.

Thus, studies to capture radicals were done to investigate the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Ni4 nanoparticles. Scavengers (IPA as an ⋅OH scavenger) were introduced to the reaction system while maintaining the other parameters. As shown in Fig. 10 , the introduction of IPA caused a clear reduction in absorption while having a slight effect on absorption.

These findings suggested that the peroxidase-like catalytic reaction for Ni4 NP, which would oxidize TMB to give a blue color, depended heavily on the strong oxidizing reaction . OH radicals formed at the time of the catalytic reaction. Owing to this, Scheme 1 depicted the peroxidase-like mechanism of the NiMn2O4 system based on the analyses above and other results [52]. The oxidation of TMB by H2O2 is catalyzed by the negatively charged NiMn2O4 nanoparticles, which function as a peroxidase mimic. More positively charged TMB molecules are collected as electron donors on the surface of NiMn2O4 throughout the reaction step. Then, thesurface of NiMn2O4 would receive the lone-pair electrons from the amino groups donated by the TMB molecules, boosting theelectron density of materialsand movement, hastening the transfer of electrons from NiMn2O4 to H2O2, and accelerating the catalytic rate of the reaction of TMB oxidation by H2O2 [63]. The coloration of the systemaltered from colorless to blue as a result of TMB being oxidized by OH radicals produced as a consequence of the interactionbetween NiMn2O4 and H2O2. As a result, NiMn2O4 can be used to construct high-sensitivity techniques for application in environmental and biological experiments due to its outstanding peroxidase mimeticactivity [52].

The concentrations of the substrates and the conditions of the reaction affect the function of natural enzymes. The effects of H2O2 concentrations, TMB concentrations, catalyst concentrations, temperature, and pH on the peroxidase-like activity of the NiMn2O4 nanoparticles were examined like those of natural enzymes. Moreover, studies to trap radicals were done to investigate the peroxidase-like catalytic process of the Ni4 nanosphere material. The Ni4 nanosphered catalyst can catalyze the emergence of an oxidized intermediate when H2O2 is involved. OH radicals, which might subsequently be utilized to oxidize TMB to create a blue dye. They confirmed the catalytic mechanism using the Ni4-TMB-H2O2 system, which IPA would readily react with. OH radical scavenger was utilized, which causes the color of the system to diminish and the absorption at 652 nm to drop [52,63,66].
The related chemical formula was:
CH3CH(OH)CH3 +.OH → CH3CH(O)CH3 + H2O.

Fig. 10 demonstrates a noticeable color fading and a drop in absorption at 652 nm for the system with IPA, supporting the evidence that the Ni4 nanosphere catalyst was capable of catalytically activating H2O2 to produceOH radicals, which were then used to oxidize TMB to yield a TMB oxide with a blue color. These findings support the crucial role of.OH in the catalytic reaction.

To explore more about the basic catalytic activities of Ni4, additional experiments have been done using various concentrations of spherical Ni4 NPs and H2O2. Related to other nanoparticle-based peroxidase mimics, the catalytic activity of Ni4 was strongly correlated with the concentration of peroxidase mimics, the concentration of H2O2, pH, and temperature. By observing the time-dependent absorption spectra at several catalyst concentrations, the impact of the catalyst concentrations was also assessed. The peroxidase-like activity of a spherical Ni4 catalyst was shown to be dependent on catalyst concentrations by the sharp increase in reaction rate with increasing catalyst concentrations. As the concentration of Ni4 nanospherical catalyst increased from 0 to 40 μg/mL−1, as depicted in Fig. 11 a, the absorbance at 652 nm steadily increased. Results in Fig. 11a also showed that the color of the solution became deeper and deeper with an increased Ni4 nanosphere concentration. Throughout the studies that followed, the optimum catalyst concentration was determined to be 20 μg/mL−1 from an efficiency and cost perspective. The peroxidase mimicking activity of Ni4 nanoparticles was reliant on the H2O2 concentrations, as indicated in Fig. 11b, where the rate of reaction accelerated as the H2O2 concentrations increased. According to earlier research, the catalytic activities of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) could be reduced by excessive concentrations of H2O2 [63,67]. The time-course-dependent absorbance change at 652 nm for various H2O2 concentrations is shown in Fig. 11b. The catalytic reaction rates will increase as the H2O2 concentration rises, and there is no suppression in the catalytic process when H2O2 levels are higher. This implies that at higher H2O2 concentrations, the enzyme catalytic activity of Ni4 nanospherical material is more stable than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [52,67].

In further investigations, the optimum H2O2 concentration was set at 4.0 mM. Similar to real horseradish peroxidase, the peroxidase-like catalytic performance of Ni4 nanoparticles can be regulated by the pH of the buffer and the temperature of the reaction (HRP). Since the impacts of temperature and pH on the catalytic activity of Ni4 nanoparticles were examined, As shown in Fig. 11c, as the buffer pH rises from 2 to 7, the enzyme activity changes and reaches its maximum at pH 4. The reason for the drop in absorbance at higher pH can be attributed to the restriction of H2O2 decomposition when the buffer pH further rises [61]. As per Fig. 11c, a pH value of 4.0 or above lowered the peroxidase-like action, whereas pH = 4.0 showed the highest absorption for the Ni4 nanosphere catalyst system. Therefore, 4.0 was the optimum pH. Additionally, the temperature has a considerable impact on the catalytic activity of Ni4. As shown in Fig. 11d, the catalytic activity increased initially as the temperature climbed and then reduced as the temperature exceeded 40 °C. Consequently, 25 °C was chosen as the appropriate temperature due to its comfort for operation.

By changing the concentration of one substrate while keeping the concentration of the other substrate constant, the steady-state kinetics were investigated to further investigate the peroxidase-like property of the Ni4 nanosphere catalyst. The kinetic parameters were determined in accordance with the Michaelis-Menten Eq. (3) and the derived Lineweaver-Burk plot [52]:

where [S] is the substrate concentration, [V] is the origin reaction velocity, [Vm] is the highest reaction velocity, and Km is the Michaelis-Menten constant. For both TMB and H2O2, the Michaelis-Menten curves were found at a specific concentration level, as shown in Fig. 12 . The catalytic reaction resembling peroxidase relied on the Ni4 nanosphere catalyst was influenced by the concentrations of substrate and exhibited the Michaelis-Menten characteristic towards both TMB and H2O2, as shown in Fig. 12 (a and b) [52]. To do this, the number of catalytic reaction parameters was estimated from the Michaelis-Menten modeling and then implemented into the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot (Fig. 12c and d) using Eq. [3]. Table 2 provides a comparison between the kinetic parameters derived from the Michaelis-Menten and Lineweaver-Burk plots and recent literature publications. As is common knowledge, Km measures the affinity of an enzyme for its substrates. The greater the affinity between the enzyme and the substrate, the smaller the value of Km [68]. The reported Km (0.168 mM) value of Ni4 nanoparticles with H2O2 substrate was much lower than that of the naturally occurring horseradish peroxidase enzyme [69] (Table 2), indicating the catalyst's stronger affinity for H2O2. The Ni4 nanosphere surface may have more active sites available than the surface of the normal horseradish peroxidase, which only contains one active site per molecule, explaining why the Km value was lower [70].

Additionally, the Ni4 nanosphere catalyst had a larger Km value with TMB than it appears to have done with HRP (Table 2), implying a decreased affinity for TMB compared to HRP. Fig. 12c and d depict double reciprocal graphs of initial velocity against a single substrate concentration to show how a typical ping-pong mechanism might react. These findings demonstrate that the Ni4 nanosphere catalyst behaved similarly to HRP in that it bound to and interacted with the initial substrate, released the first product, and then reacted with the second substrate [52,71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 based on Ni4 nanosphere peroxidase mimics
ce:section.ce:para: An easy and feasible colorimetric approach for the perception of H2O2 was developed on the basis of the inherent peroxidase-similar enzymatic performance of Ni4 nanoparticles that was previously stated. Fig. 13 a displays the time-course-dependent absorbance variations of oxidized TMB at 652 nm in the presence of various amounts of H2O2. It is evident that the concentration of H2O2 was increased from 1.0 μM to 0.25 mM, and the reaction rate has increased. Furthermore, the standard H2O2 concentration–response curve (Fig. 13 (a-b)) displayed a linear response ranging from 2 μM to 0.25 mM, and the detection limit of H2O2 was 2 μM. Aside from this, the linear regression equation is 652 nm = 0.63477 ± 0.00617, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9922. Ni4 nanospheres exhibited greater catalytic activity than many previously disclosed peroxidase mimics based on nanoparticles. (Table 2) [52,63,72]. These findings showed that the Ni4 nanosphere-based H2O2 detection system was not only an easy and effective optical technique but also a precise approach to identifying H2O2, offering a superior foundation for H2O2 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of Rh B and MB
ce:section.ce:para: Studies have shown that enzymes can help clean up wastewater from contaminants [73–75]. The outstanding capacity to mimic peroxidase was thoroughly utilized to study their degradability, utilizing the dyes MB (Methylene Blue) and Rhodamine B (Rh B) in the presence of H2O2 and under the influence of visible light. To facilitate surface adsorption, metal oxide (20 mg), 50 mL of MB, Rh B at 5 mg/L, and 10 mL of 30% H2O2 were added to a beaker. The solution was then maintained and exposed to visible light after that. The maximal UV–Vis absorption in the aqueous solutions of MB and Rh B occurs at 664 nm and 553 nm, respectively. With longer reaction times, there is a sharp decline in the absorption of MB and Rh B, and the bands at 664 and 553 nm become weak and broad. Compared to an aqueous MB solution, the Ni4nanosphere hasa higher capacity to degrade in an aqueous Rh B solution. After every 30 min, the absorption intensity reduced, displaying the change in concentration of the dye molecule. The UV–vis absorption spectra of MB and Rh B dyes in an aqueous solution when Ni4 and H2O2 are involved are shown in Fig. 14 (a and b) at different reaction times. The absorption of MB and Rh B drops as reaction times increase, and the band at 664 and 553 nm becomes exceedingly thin and broad. Compared to an aqueous MB solution, the Ni4 nanosphere has a higher capacity to degrade in an aqueous Rh B solution. With progressive irradiation, the intensity peak λ max of Rh B was slightly reduced without any discernible shift in the absorption wavelength. This implies that degradation does not occur through demethylation.

Determining the point of zero charges permits a large portion of the charge upon that NP's surfaces to be predicted throughout the photodegradation process (pHPZC) [76]. Since photocatalysis occurs on the NP's surface, the pH of the solution, the kind of pollutant, and the capacity of the surface to bind the pollutant all have a substantial impact on the effectiveness of the photocatalyst [77,78]. The surface characteristics of the NiMn2O4 NPs can change depending on the pH. The pH of the solution has an impact on dye photodegradation. The surface charge of NiMn2O4 nanoparticles and the potentials of catalytic processes alter in response to changes in solution pH. As a consequence, the ability of the dye to bind to the surface is altered, altering the reaction rate. The surface of the photocatalyst charge depends on pH. When the pH of the solution is at the isoelectric point (point of zero charges), the least amount of dye can be absorbed. Below the isoelectric point, the photocatalyst's surface is positively charged while carrying a negative charge over it. The H+ and OH– ions in the medium affect the surface charge of the nanoparticles. The pH of an aqueous solution at which the total of all surface positive charges matches the total of all surface negative charges is known as the point of zero charges, or pzc. To determine the pHPZC of the Ni1, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4 NPs, a standard set of titrations was undertaken. The electrolyte used for the titrations was a 0.1 M NaCl solution. The graph, which is depicted in Fig. 15 , plotted pH against ΔpH. The pHPZC (point of zero charges) of Ni1, Ni2, Ni3, and Ni4 materials was estimated at 7.15, 7.2, 7.3, and 7.5, respectively, using the reported method [78]. This finding suggests the material is positively charged in the solution pH < pHPZC and negatively charged in the solution pH > pHPZC. In the case of NiMn2O4 nanoparticles, higher pH values promote the formation of a negative charge on the NPs. Nanospherical Ni4 nanoparticles have a more homogenous surface with a consistent distribution of surface functional groups. The uniformity in surface properties can lead to a more predictable and consistent pHPZC compared to agglomerated morphologies of Ni1, Ni2, and Ni3 materials. Lower-sized Ni4 nanomaterials with higher surface curvature also experience stronger electrostatic interactions with ions in the surrounding solution, leading to differences in surface charge compared to larger agglomerated nanoparticles.

Thus, the positively charged cationic dyes MB and Rh B can be adsorbed on the surface of the strongly negatively charged Ni4 nanoparticles through a strong electrostatic attraction. The electrostatic interaction was advantageous for boosting the adsorptive features, thereby expanding the degradation efficiency improvements, as in the cases of NiMn2O4 nanoparticles prepared at varying concentrations of PEG. On the other hand, Ni1 synthesized without PEG (plain) will exhibit lower efficiency as compared with Ni4 nanoparticles due to a lower negative charge on the surface than Ni4, and it will create a slight electrostatic repulsion between Ni1 and the cationic dyes. These results are fully consistent with the previously published reports [79].

The degradation efficiency of MB and Rh B was estimated by employing equation (4)): [68].

where is the absorbency of MB (664 nm) and Rh B (553 nm) dyes in aqueous solution at different intervals and is the concentration of the dyes at various intervals. The degradation rates of MB and Rh B are 68 and 80.7%, respectively, after 210 min. Comparing Ni4 nanoparticles to MB dye, Rh B degradation by Ni4 nanoparticles is greater by over 12%. Fig. 14(a–d) displays the corresponding degree of degradation curve. According to the figure, after 210 min, Rh B degraded almost completely in comparison to the MB dye solution. The shift in the adsorption percentage is caused by the electrostatic attractions among MB dye and the surfaces of Ni4 NPs [80]. Electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged Ni4 (pHPZC = 7.5) and the surface of the Ni4 NPs was predominant, which increased the MB and Rh B adsorption on the surface of the NPs. The catalyst Ni4 repels the MB and Rh B particles in an acidic medium because MB and Rh B are basic dyes that generate cations in water. Hence, degradation was less in an acidic medium. On the other hand, the catalyst in the basic medium is negatively charged, making adsorption possible and increasing the rate of degradation. Additionally, the rate of deterioration increases in an alkaline environment due to the ease with which –OH radicals can be generated [81]. Rh B photo-degradation interacts more electrostatically than MB does with Ni4 nanoparticles, which causes the degradation to proceed more quickly. Rh B also has more active interaction sites (N, O, COOH, and Ph rings) on its surface than MB (S and Ph rings). These results were also used to perform a kinetic analysis of MB and Rh B dye in the presence of Ni4 nanoparticles, as depicted in Fig. 16 (a–c). A pseudo-first-order reaction model was used to confirm the rate of MB and Rh B dye degradation by the Ni4 Nanoparticles [1,82].

The rate constant of Ni4 Nanoparticles is nearly half a fold higher in R-B dye as compared with MB dye solution. From these results, Ni4 nanoparticles have higher photocatalytic activity towards Rh B as compared with the MB dye solution.

In Scheme 1, a potential photocatalytic route has been devised to explain the H2O2-mediated photocatalytic activity. The electrons in the valence band (VB) of the NiMn2O4 (Ni4) Nanosphere photocatalyst move to the conduction band (CB), yielding a similar number of holes in the VB as seen in Scheme 3. When combined with dissolved oxygen, photogenerated electrons can create superoxide radical anions (O2), while photogenerated holes can turn HO– into HO. Radical conversion. In photocatalysis, the combination of holes (h+) and electrons (e) has been viewed as a detrimental process. Thus, by delaying the recombination procedure or enhancing photocatalysis efficacy in the presence of additional catalysts, the plausibility of the photocatalytic activity has been raised. Here, H2O2 can function as an electron acceptor in this condition, which might cause the creation of hydroxyl radicals by using the following reaction: Initially, the major rate-enhancing mechanism in this process is the rapid photolysis of H2O2·H2O2 is advised to be a superior electron acceptor to oxygen in another modest mechanism that may partially affect rate acceleration. As indicated in Eq. ii, this can yield one hydroxyl radical instead of the weaker O2 radical, minimizing the chance of electron-hole recombination. Lastly, the major element responsible for the degradation of the MB and Rh B dye molecules is represented by these generated radicals (HO., O2.-).

Catalyst stability and recycling are crucial factors. The Ni4 nanoparticles were whirled in order to prepare them for the following investigations after the photocatalytic reaction procedure. As seen in Fig. 17 . Ni4 nanoparticles remain at high efficiency after five cycles. Ni4 nanoparticles had a degradation rate of about 99.9% for RhB photocatalytic degradation in the first test and 98% for five cycles, and about 98% for MB photocatalytic degradation in the initial test and 97% for five cycles. This outcome indicated that Ni4 nanoparticles have relatively high durability and stabilization.

The superior photocatalytic activity of Ni4 nanosphere catalysts is explained as follows:
1.
The introduction of PEG not only stabilized the small particles but also inhibited their agglomeration. The spherical morphology and porosity of the Ni4 nanoparticles prepared with a high concentration of PEG possessed absorption of light in the visible region and the band gap (1.73 eV), revealing they could initiate photocatalysis effectively.
2.
Owing to their high specific surface area and pore volume, Ni4 nanoparticles provide more reaction sites, enhancing colorant adsorption and several surface-adsorbed reactants, increasing the photocatalytic activity.
3.
The strong electrostatic attraction allows the positively charged cationic dyes MB and RhB to attach to the surface of the intensely negatively charged Ni4 nanoparticles, and the electrostatic interaction proved invaluable for improving the adsorptive characteristic and accelerating the deterioration.

This study revealedthat a facile, PEG-mediated hydrothermal approach had been employedto synthesize the spinel-structured NiMn2O4 nanoparticles. The nanospherical structure is obtained with a higher amount of PEG-6000 surfactant. The better specific surface area and nano-spherical shape would provide many reaction sites for photocatalytic processes. The higher specific surface area of Ni4 offered more active sites, which led to a rise in the intrinsic peroxidase mimic activity and catalyzed TMB into blue ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2. The synthesized Ni4 nanoparticles had a greater catalytic capacity than the HRP natural enzyme. A higher affinity of the catalyst for H2O2 was indicated by the calculated Km value of Ni4 nanoparticles with the H2O2 substrate (0.168 mM), which was much shorter than that of the normal horseradish peroxidase enzyme. A facile and sensitive visualsystem for H2O2 monitoring was developed using Ni4 nanoparticles, making them a perceived competence for realistic H2O2 measurement. Furthermore, using the results of the pHPZE determination, the impact of the surface charge of the NPs on the photocatalytic performance was examined. The band gap (1.73 eV) and visible light absorption of Ni4 nanoparticles also demonstrated their ability to efficiently start photocatalysis. Additionally, the photocatalytic activity of Ni4 nanoparticles on the degradation of MB and RhB dyes has also been investigated. The findings show that RhB dye has a higher photocatalytic efficacy than MB. The cationic dyes MB and RhB with positive charges can be adsorbed on the surface of the highly negatively charged Ni4 nanoparticles through a strong electrostatic interaction, thereby improving the adsorptive characteristics and enhancing degradation. The recyclable ability of Ni4 nanoparticles had a degradation rate of about 99% for RhB photocatalytic degradation in the first test and 98% for five cycles, and about 98% for MB photocatalytic degradation in the first test and 97% for five cycles. These results demonstrated that the present Ni4 nanoparticles might offer some fresh perspectives on the search for novel enzyme mimics with improved photocatalytic activity.
T. Shahanas: Data curation, Writing – original draft. G. Harichandran: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision.
